reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Strong opening that explains what a batch job is and why a first-time user would choose it.
Good early decision framing with clear When to use / When NOT to use sections and concrete alternatives.
Copyable examples are practical and the page covers the main batch-job levers: compute, triggers, retries, Spot, logging, and resource access.
The FAQ is unusually useful: it has exactly 10 questions and mixes Stacktape-specific, AWS, and comparison questions.
problems: H2 ordering drift: the shared sections do not match the example's order. `Container image`/packaging appears before `Compute resources`, which breaks the section rhythm used in the sibling page.

The `Container image` section overstates support by asserting that batch jobs use the same five packaging modes as container workloads. The provided batch-job source only shows `container.packaging` and does not expose the union here, so this should be narrowed or linked out instead of stated as fact.

The `Triggers` section uses the broad phrase `storage events`; the source is specific about S3, SNS, SQS, Kinesis, DynamoDB streams, CloudWatch Logs, schedules, and EventBridge. Use the concrete event-source names for precision.
mandatoryFixes: Reorder H2s so shared sections appear in the same order as the example. Move `Compute resources` ahead of `Container image`/packaging, and keep the remaining shared sections in the example's relative order.

Replace the unsupported packaging claim with source-grounded wording. Either state only that a batch job runs a container image with configurable `container.packaging`, or link to packaging docs without asserting the full mode list from this source.
optionalImprovements: Replace `storage events` with the exact batch-job event sources named in the source files.
Tighten the `Container image` section so it gives one clear recommendation for most users and one clear reason to switch packaging modes.
Consider adding one sentence that distinguishes batch jobs from worker services in terms of lifecycle: batch jobs start, do work, and exit; worker services stay running.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: Strong opening and early decision guidance: the page clearly distinguishes batch jobs from web-service, private-service, worker-service, and Lambda function.
Stacktape config examples use the required class-based TypeScript style inside <CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]} />, with no hand-authored YAML config.

The page covers the core production levers for this resource: container packaging, compute sizing, triggers, timeout, retries, Spot capacity, resource access, logging, API reference, and referenceable parameters.
Spot behavior is grounded and useful: it names the up-to-90% savings claim, interruption risk, SIGTERM behavior, 120-second shutdown window, and idempotency tradeoff.
FAQ is exactly 10 Q&A pairs and mixes Stacktape-specific, AWS/use-case, and comparison questions.
problems: Mandatory example-guided drift: the shared H2 order differs from the example. The example places Compute resources before Packaging; this draft places Container image/packaging before Compute resources.

The pricing context and FAQ say batch jobs use Fargate-style vCPU and memory pricing, but the BatchJob source describes AWS auto-provisioning a matching instance and has a commented-out EC2 instanceTypes note. Pricing source says Fargate is used by batch-job, so this is probably acceptable, but the wording should stay cautious and avoid implying every batch job is literally an always-running Fargate task.

The Triggers section enumerates all supported integration types, but it does not mention key source-backed operational gotchas for several trigger families, such as SQS single-consumer guidance, stream batch retry behavior, and CloudWatch Logs payload encoding. This page is still useful, but production readers may miss failure-mode implications.

The Compute resources section uses a GPU example but does not anchor the specific `cpu: 8`, `memory: 30720`, and `gpu: 1` values enough. It explains the fields, but not why that sizing was chosen or that the source does not expose valid numeric ranges.
The Logging section lists retention values but omits `5`, which is included in the source union. This is small, but it weakens trust in a page aimed at configuration accuracy.
mandatoryFixes: Reorder shared H2s so sections shared with the example keep the same relative order. Move the packaging/container-image section after Compute resources, or otherwise preserve the example's Compute resources before Packaging order.
optionalImprovements: Add one cautious sentence in Compute resources explaining that the source requires `cpu` and `memory`, supports optional `gpu`, and does not document allowed numeric ranges on this page; readers should use API Reference for exact accepted values if validation changes.

In Triggers, add short failure-mode notes for high-risk sources: SQS batch/idempotency, Kinesis/DynamoDB whole-batch retries, CloudWatch Logs gzip/base64 payloads, and EventBridge/SNS delivery-failure destinations where configured.
Change the Logging retention list to include every value from the source union, or avoid enumerating the full list and point readers to the API Reference for exhaustive values.
Consider adding an Info callout for the pricing context or Spot tradeoff if maintaining closer MDX rhythm with the example matters, but this is not a blocker because the current prose is clear.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Strong decision-first structure: the page answers when to use a batch job, when not to use it, and then moves into the knobs readers actually care about.
The pricing context is near the top and is grounded in the provided pricing summary, which makes the page immediately useful for cost-sensitive readers.
The examples are mostly realistic and copyable, especially the schedule trigger, Spot retry pattern, and `connectTo` example.
The FAQ is broad and well-formed: it covers Stacktape-specific questions, AWS behavior, and comparison questions instead of staying too narrow.
problems: The `When to use` section mentions `instanceTypes` as if batch jobs support a narrower instance override, but the provided `BatchJobResources` source only documents `cpu`, `memory`, and optional `gpu`; `instanceTypes` is commented out in the source.
The logging section enumerates `retentionDays` incompletely and incorrectly; the source allows `1, 3, 5, 7, 14, 30, 60, 90, 120, 150, 180, 365, 400, 545, 731, 1827, 3653`, not just the subset shown.

The `connectTo` example introduces unrelated database tunables (`engine: new RdsEnginePostgres({ version: '16' })` and `instanceClass: 'db.t4g.micro'`) without any prose explaining why those values are there.
Several `When NOT to use` comparisons rely on features of other resources that are not grounded in the batch-job source you provided, especially the `web-service` feature list.
The page drifts from the reviewed example's MDX palette by putting pricing in bold prose instead of using the callout pattern the sibling page uses for pricing context.
mandatoryFixes: Remove the `instanceTypes` wording from the `When to use` bullet; the provided source does not document batch-job instance overrides.
Fix the logging retention sentence so it either lists the full supported `retentionDays` set from the source or stops enumerating values in prose.
Either simplify the `connectTo` example or add a short explanation of the database tunables it contains, so the example stays focused on batch-job access patterns.
Tighten the `When NOT to use` comparisons to only claim behavior that is sourced here, or move the extra resource behavior into linked cross-references instead of stating it as fact in this page.
optionalImprovements: Move the pricing note into an `<Info>` callout to match the sibling page's pricing pattern and keep the section palette consistent.
Consider adding one short sentence in the Spot section that says the default is on-demand and when that default is still the better choice.
The packaging section is useful as a comparison table, but a short lead-in sentence calling out the most common mode would make the decision path faster to scan.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is mostly grounded in the provided BatchJob, event, packaging, connectTo, logging, and pricing sources. I found a few publication-relevant issues: one broken or unsupported cross-link, one likely unsupported packaging claim for batch-job private image credentials, one over-specific cost model statement, and one minor wording mismatch around the old `stacktape logs` source text.

issues.statement: The draft links to `[cost optimization](/managing-costs/optimization-tips)`, but that route is not present in the provided navigation index. The valid route is `/managing-costs/optimization-tips` only if it appears exactly in the navigation index; verify spelling before publishing.
issues.reason: Invented or mismatched documentation routes are publication blockers under the prompt rules. In the provided navigation index, the listed route is `/managing-costs/optimization-tips`, so if the draft route is exactly that it is valid; otherwise this should be corrected. I am flagging this only to force exact-route verification because route mismatches are high impact.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the link only if it exactly matches `/managing-costs/optimization-tips`; otherwise change it to the exact route from the navigation index or remove the link.

issues.statement: “Stacktape supports the same five container packaging modes used by container workloads: Stacktape image buildpack, custom Dockerfile, prebuilt image, Nixpacks, and external buildpack.”
issues.reason: The provided sources show batch-job-specific packaging interfaces for these five modes, but the draft says they are “the same” as container workloads. The sources show batch-job variants have different prop shapes from container-workload variants, for example prebuilt batch-job images do not expose `repositoryCredentialsSecretArn` or `entryPoint` while container-workload prebuilt images do. The practical list of modes is supported, but “same” overstates equivalence.
issues.suggestedFix: Write: “A batch job supports five container image packaging modes: Stacktape image buildpack, custom Dockerfile, prebuilt image, Nixpacks, and external buildpack. These are the batch-job packaging variants; some options differ from long-running container workloads.”

issues.statement: “Batch jobs use vCPU and memory billing while the job runs.”
issues.reason: The pricing source provided for batch jobs is explicitly an AWS Fargate pricing summary, but the BatchJob type source says AWS auto-provisions a matching instance and supports GPU workloads. The draft’s sentence makes the entire batch-job pricing model sound only like Fargate vCPU/memory billing, which is narrower than the BatchJob resource description and may be incomplete for GPU-backed jobs.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the pricing line: “For Fargate-backed batch jobs, the provided pricing data uses vCPU and memory billing while the job runs…” Keep GPU cost wording qualitative unless a provided source includes GPU prices.

evidence.quote:
**AWS Fargate** (used by `web-service`, `private-service`, `worker-service`, `multi-container-workload`, `batch-job`):
- vCPU billing: ~$7.29/month per 0.25 up to ~$466/month per 16.
- Memory billing: ~$1.60/month per 512 MB up to ~$384/month per 122880 MB.

* Pay only for the compute time used. Supports CPU and GPU workloads, retries on failure,
 * and can be triggered by schedules, HTTP requests, S3 uploads, or queue messages.

issues.statement: “View runtime logs with [`stacktape debug:logs`](/cli/debug-logs) or in the [Stacktape Console](/stacktape-console/console-overview).”
issues.reason: The `stacktape debug:logs` command and route are required by the prompt and are valid, but the provided source file for BatchJob logging says logs are “viewable with `stacktape logs`,” not `debug:logs`, and the provided source files do not include Console behavior showing batch-job logs. The CLI wording is acceptable under the prompt’s canonical rule, but the Console claim is not grounded in the supplied Console source because no Console source was provided.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the `stacktape debug:logs` link because the prompt requires it, but either remove “or in the Stacktape Console” or phrase it cautiously only if a Console source is provided elsewhere.

evidence.quote: * #### Container logging (stdout/stderr). Sent to CloudWatch, viewable with `stacktape logs`.

issues.statement: “Stacktape lets AWS pick a matching instance unless the API reference exposes a narrower option for your use case.”
issues.reason: The source says AWS auto-provisions a matching instance and includes a commented-out `instanceTypes` property, but the active `BatchJobResources` interface does not expose `instanceTypes`. The phrase “unless the API reference exposes” is cautious, but it may confuse readers by implying there may be a current configurable instance-type option on this page.
issues.suggestedFix: Simplify to the active documented behavior: “Stacktape lets AWS select matching compute from the declared CPU, memory, and optional GPU requirements.”

issues.statement: “With `30` and `2`, waits grow from 30 seconds to 60 seconds to 120 seconds.”
issues.reason: The source defines `retryIntervalSeconds` and `retryIntervalMultiplier` and gives a similar example, but it does not define whether the final listed wait occurs after the third failed attempt or before a fourth attempt. With `attempts: 3`, listing three waits may imply more retry delays than the number of attempts would produce.
issues.suggestedFix: Avoid tying the sequence to the exact `attempts: 3` example. Write: “With `retryIntervalSeconds: 30` and `retryIntervalMultiplier: 2`, each subsequent retry delay doubles.”

positiveFindings: The draft uses `defineConfig` and class-based TypeScript Stacktape config examples inside `<CodeBlock intellisense>` with the required `label: 'TypeScript'`.
`<ApiReference definitionName="BatchJobProps" />` matches the page metadata type name requirement and uses the `Props` suffix.

The trigger list matches the `BatchJobProps.events` union in `batch-jobs.d.ts`, including HTTP API Gateway, ALB, S3, SNS, SQS, Kinesis, DynamoDB streams, CloudWatch Logs, schedules, and EventBridge event buses.
The retry defaults, logging defaults, Spot interruption warning, connectTo environment variable pattern, and referenceable parameters are grounded in the provided type sources.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is mostly well grounded in the provided BatchJob types, trigger types, packaging props, connectTo behavior, logging defaults, retry defaults, and pricing source. The main publication blockers are unsupported or wrong claims around specific connectTo environment variable names and a couple of claims that rely on properties or capabilities not exposed for batch jobs in the provided source.

issues.statement: "With the resource names above, the job receives database variables such as `STP_MAIN_DATABASE_CONNECTION_STRING`, `STP_MAIN_DATABASE_HOST`, and `STP_MAIN_DATABASE_PORT`, plus bucket variables such as `STP_REPORTS_BUCKET_NAME` and `STP_REPORTS_BUCKET_ARN`."
issues.reason: The database variable examples correctly match `mainDatabase`, but the bucket resource is named `reportsBucket`, so the generated env vars should include the full normalized resource name, not `REPORTS_BUCKET`. The source defines the pattern as `STP_[RESOURCE_NAME]_[PARAM]`.
issues.suggestedFix: Change the bucket examples to `STP_REPORTS_BUCKET_NAME` only if the example resource is renamed to `reports`; otherwise use the normalized `reportsBucket` name, such as `STP_REPORTS_BUCKET_NAME` only if Stacktape's documented normalization proves that exact acronym split. The safest fix is to rename the resource to `reports` and use `STP_REPORTS_NAME` / `STP_REPORTS_ARN`, or avoid concrete bucket env var names and state the pattern plus provided params.

evidence.quote: Injects environment variables with connection details: `STP_[RESOURCE_NAME]_[PARAM]`

issues.reason: `iamRoleStatements` exists on `ResourceAccessProps`, but the page's example code never demonstrates the shape. The claim itself is supported; however the surrounding sentence implies it is a direct alternative for permissions not covered by `connectTo`, which is supported by the source. No change required for this exact sentence.
issues.suggestedFix: No fix needed; this is retained as a positive finding, not a blocking issue.

issues.statement: "Use `$Secret()` or `$ResourceParam()` when a value should come from Stacktape directives instead of being hard-coded; see directives for the supported directive model."
issues.reason: The batch job container environment source explicitly supports `$ResourceParam()` and `$Secret()`, so this is grounded. No issue.

issues.statement: "A batch job always runs a container image. Stacktape supports the same five container packaging modes used by container workloads: Stacktape image buildpack, custom Dockerfile, prebuilt image, Nixpacks, and external buildpack."
issues.reason: The provided source shows `BatchJobContainer.packaging: BatchJobContainerPackaging` and defines batch-job packaging prop interfaces for prebuilt image, custom Dockerfile, external buildpack, Nixpacks, and Stacktape image buildpack. This is grounded. No issue.

issues.statement: "A batch job can start from several event sources: HTTP API Gateway, Application Load Balancer, S3, SNS, SQS, Kinesis, DynamoDB streams, CloudWatch Logs, schedules, and EventBridge event buses."
issues.reason: The union in `BatchJobProps.events` contains each listed integration. No issue.

positiveFindings: The page uses `ApiReference definitionName="BatchJobProps"`, which matches the page metadata and the source type name.
The draft correctly keeps "When to use" and "When NOT to use" as peer H2 sections.
The Stacktape configuration examples use `<CodeBlock intellisense>` with a `TypeScript` tab and avoid hand-authored YAML.
The retry defaults are correctly grounded: `attempts` defaults to `1`, `retryIntervalSeconds` to `0`, and `retryIntervalMultiplier` to `1`.
The logging defaults are correctly grounded: CloudWatch logging can be disabled with `logging.disabled`, and `retentionDays` defaults to `90`.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is broadly complete for BatchJobProps: it covers the core resource shape, major operational decisions, packaging variants, trigger variants, retries, Spot behavior, logging, connectTo, API reference, and referenceable params. I found one medium completeness issue around a documented union/enum property whose supported values are only partially named.

issues.statement: `retentionDays` accepts the CloudWatch retention values exposed in the API reference, including `1`, `3`, `7`, `14`, `30`, `60`, `90`, `180`, `365`, and longer archival periods.
issues.reason: The draft documents `logging.retentionDays`, which is a literal union in the source, but only names some values. Because the sentence says “including” and points to the API reference, this is not factually wrong, but it is incomplete under union-variant coverage: the source also supports `5`, `120`, `150`, `400`, `545`, `731`, `1827`, and `3653`.
issues.suggestedFix: Either avoid partial enumeration and say “Use the API reference for the full set of supported CloudWatch retention values,” or name the full source union: `1`, `3`, `5`, `7`, `14`, `30`, `60`, `90`, `120`, `150`, `180`, `365`, `400`, `545`, `731`, `1827`, and `3653`.

evidence.quote: `retentionDays` accepts the CloudWatch retention values exposed in the API reference, including `1`, `3`, `7`, `14`, `30`, `60`, `90`, `180`, `365`, and longer archival periods.

positiveFindings: The draft mentions every important BatchJobProps property likely to matter to most users: `container`, `resources`, `timeout`, `useSpotInstances`, `logging`, `retryConfig`, `events`, `connectTo`, and `iamRoleStatements`.
The container packaging union is acknowledged with all five batch-job container packaging modes: Stacktape image buildpack, custom Dockerfile, prebuilt image, Nixpacks, and external buildpack.

The `events` union is covered with all BatchJob-supported trigger types from the source: HTTP API Gateway, Application Load Balancer, S3, SNS, SQS, Kinesis, DynamoDB streams, CloudWatch Logs, schedules, and EventBridge event buses.

Each `<CodeBlock intellisense>` example demonstrates its section topic: scheduled events in Basic example, `resources.gpu` in Compute resources, `retryConfig` and `timeout` in Retries and timeout, `useSpotInstances` in Spot capacity, `connectTo` in Connecting to resources, and `logging.retentionDays` in Logging.
The page uses exactly one `<ApiReference definitionName="BatchJobProps" />`, matching the metadata typeName.

reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Strong opening definition and a useful pricing context near the top.
The page covers the main decision points first: when to use, when not to use, compute sizing, triggers, retries, Spot capacity, logging, and resource access.
The basic example is realistic and copyable, and the later sections use tables and short headings well.
The FAQ has the required breadth and gives the reader a fast path to common questions.
problems: The `When NOT to use` section compares batch jobs to `web-service`, `private-service`, `worker-service`, and `Lambda function` using feature claims that are not grounded in the provided source bundle.
The Packaging section says the listed options 'differ from long-running container workloads,' but the provided batch-job source does not establish that distinction.
Several FAQ answers repeat the same unsupported comparison pattern, especially the `worker-service` and `Lambda function` answers.
mandatoryFixes: Rewrite the `When NOT to use` section so every alternative claim is backed by the provided source files, or replace those bullets with source-grounded, minimal comparisons.
Trim or reword the Packaging section so it does not claim unsupported differences from long-running container workloads.
Apply the same grounding pass to the FAQ answers that compare batch-job to `worker-service`, `web-service`, and `Lambda function`.
optionalImprovements: Tighten the pricing paragraph so it explicitly reads as a usage-based Fargate cost estimate, not a fixed monthly price.
Add one short sentence in the Packaging section that explains why a reader would choose each non-default packaging mode instead of the buildpack.
Mention the default `useSpotInstances: false` in the Spot section to make the opt-in tradeoff clearer for first-time readers.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: Strong overall structure that matches the reviewed example: early decision sections, basic copyable example, focused configuration sections, FAQ, API reference, and referenceable parameters.
The TypeScript Stacktape config examples follow the required class-based `defineConfig` + `<CodeBlock intellisense>` pattern, with no forbidden YAML or `StacktapeConfig` style.
Good practical warnings around retries, idempotency, Spot interruption, timeout behavior, and logging retention.
Trigger union coverage is solid and does not imply only one event source is supported.
The page correctly uses the canonical `STP_[RESOURCE_NAME]_[PARAM]` pattern and gives resource-name-specific examples for `mainDatabase` and `reports`.
problems: The pricing context calls the resource “Fargate-backed batch jobs,” but the source primarily says AWS auto-provisions matching compute for batch jobs and the pricing file only says Fargate is used by batch-job. The wording may overstate the compute model unless runtime code confirms every batch job is Fargate-backed.

The opening pricing sentence gives continuous monthly prices but does not clearly say batch jobs bill only while running. A production reader could misread the monthly number as an always-on floor rather than a normalized continuous-usage figure.

The `Spot capacity` section says interrupted jobs are “resubmitted instead of ending as failed work” when combined with retries. The source says `retryConfig` auto-retries on interruption, but the wording should stay closer to “retried” rather than implying a specific resubmission mechanism.

The `Triggers` section is useful but thin for production behavior: it names retry-prone sources and says handlers should be idempotent, but does not call out the source-backed batch retry behavior for SQS/Kinesis/DynamoDB where whole batches can be retried.

The page omits any mention that `iamRoleStatements` exists for permissions outside `connectTo` until the end of the connecting section. That is acceptable, but the section would be more actionable with one concrete example or clearer guidance for AWS services not covered by `connectTo`.

The `Logging` section says logging is enabled unless `logging.disabled` is set, which is inferred from the `@default false` on `disabled` and is likely correct, but the source wording only explicitly says stdout/stderr are sent to CloudWatch. This is a mild grounding risk, not a blocker.
optionalImprovements: Clarify the pricing line: “Batch jobs pay for compute while they run; the monthly figures below are normalized to continuous us-east-1 usage.”
Replace “Fargate-backed batch jobs” with narrower wording such as “For batch-job compute in the provided pricing data...” unless source code confirms the exact compute backend.
In `Spot capacity`, replace “resubmitted” with “retried” to match the source terminology.
Add one sentence under `Triggers` noting that SQS, Kinesis, and DynamoDB stream integrations process records in batches and failed batches should be handled idempotently.
Consider adding a short `iamRoleStatements` example only if this page is expected to cover direct AWS service access beyond Stacktape resources.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: The page opens with a clear definition plus a pricing context line, so the reader immediately knows what a batch job is and what it roughly costs to run.
The `When to use` / `When NOT to use` framing is practical and opinionated, and the basic example is realistic enough to copy into a real project.
The compute, retries, Spot, connectTo, and logging sections cover the main operational decisions a batch-job user actually needs.
The FAQ is broad, has the required 10 questions, and mixes Stacktape-specific, AWS-service, and comparison questions well.
The docs correctly use `stacktape debug:logs` and the `STP_[RESOURCE_NAME]_[PARAM]` naming pattern for injected environment variables.
problems: The `When NOT to use` bullets make detailed claims about `web-service`, `private-service`, and `worker-service` that are not grounded in the provided source set for this page.
`Triggers` reads more like a supported-values inventory than a decision guide, so a reader still has to infer when to choose schedules, queues, streams, or request-based triggers.
`Packaging` names all five modes but does not give enough guidance on which one to start with or what tradeoff should push someone away from the default buildpack.
mandatoryFixes: Rewrite the `When NOT to use` bullets so they only make claims supported by the provided source files, or narrow them to generic comparisons that do not depend on undocumented features of other Stacktape resource pages.
optionalImprovements: Add one recommendation sentence at the top of `Triggers` that groups the event sources into the most common decision paths, such as schedules, queue/stream processing, and request-driven jobs.
Add a short lead-in above the packaging table that states which mode most teams should start with and when to switch to Dockerfile, prebuilt image, Nixpacks, or external buildpack.
Consider adding a brief note in `Compute resources` that explicitly calls out the `gpu` field's effect, since it is easy to miss in the example.

verifierResults.summary: No factual blockers found. The draft is broadly grounded in the provided BatchJob, events, packaging, resource access, logging, retry, and pricing sources, and it avoids the forbidden Stacktape config patterns.
positiveFindings: The page uses `ApiReference definitionName="BatchJobProps"`, which matches the page metadata and source type name.

All Stacktape configuration examples use `<CodeBlock intellisense>` with a `TypeScript` tab and class-based `defineConfig` examples; no hand-authored YAML or plain-object `StacktapeConfig` style appears.

The event-source list matches the `BatchJobProps.events` union: HTTP API Gateway, Application Load Balancer, S3, SNS, SQS, Kinesis, DynamoDB streams, CloudWatch Logs, schedules, and EventBridge event buses.
Retry defaults, timeout behavior, Spot interruption behavior, and logging defaults are supported by `types/stacktape-config/batch-jobs.d.ts`.
The `connectTo` explanation uses the canonical `STP_[RESOURCE_NAME]_[PARAM]` pattern and concrete examples that match the resource names in the code sample.
The pricing context uses the generated Fargate pricing summary with approximate wording and does not invent additional dollar amounts.

verifierResults.summary: No concrete factual or source-grounding issues found in the draft. The page stays within the provided BatchJob, event, packaging, connectTo, logging, retry, Spot, pricing, and navigation sources, and it avoids the forbidden Stacktape config formats.
positiveFindings: Uses TypeScript class-based Stacktape config examples with `<CodeBlock intellisense>` and no hand-authored YAML.
`<ApiReference definitionName="BatchJobProps" />` matches the page resource type and uses the required Props suffix.
Trigger coverage matches the `BatchJobProps.events` union from the source.
Retry defaults, Spot behavior, logging defaults, retention values, and referenceable parameters are grounded in the provided source files.
Cross-links point to routes present in the supplied documentation navigation index.

verifierResults.summary: No API completeness, union-variant coverage, or code-example demonstrativeness issues found for the BatchJobProps draft. The page covers the important primary properties, names the documented event and packaging variants, and the focused code examples exercise the section topics they appear under.
positiveFindings: The draft mentions every important BatchJobProps property that most users would touch: container packaging/environment, resources, timeout, useSpotInstances, logging, retryConfig, events, connectTo, and iamRoleStatements.
The packaging section acknowledges all five batch-job container packaging variants shown in the source: Stacktape image buildpack, custom Dockerfile, prebuilt image, Nixpacks, and external buildpack.

The triggers section names all event union members listed for BatchJobProps: HTTP API Gateway, Application Load Balancer, S3, SNS, SQS, Kinesis, DynamoDB streams, CloudWatch Logs, schedules, and EventBridge event buses.

Each Stacktape config example is demonstrative for its section: scheduled events in Basic example, gpu/cpu/memory in Compute resources, timeout/retryConfig in Retries and timeout, useSpotInstances in Spot capacity, connectTo in Connecting to resources, and logging.retentionDays in Logging.
The page includes exactly one ApiReference component using the correct metadata type name: BatchJobProps.
